problem_frame: WhatIfInvest needs asset visuals that feel intentional and recognizable without implying official brand affiliation. The current first-letter and small-dot treatments are simple but inconsistent across app and share surfaces.

selected_approach.title: SwiftUI circular ticker badge
selected_approach.summary: Create a reusable circular AssetBadgeView that uses the existing asset tint plus ticker text, then apply it consistently across app-wide asset display surfaces.
pros: Avoids official logo licensing and trademark ambiguity
Fits the current circular badge language
Can be reused across compact, standard, and share-card contexts
Keeps the design easy to update later
cons: Ticker-only visual identity is less distinctive than real brand logos
Longer tickers such as GOOGL and AMZN need careful font scaling in small circles

alternatives.title: SwiftUI circular ticker badge
alternatives.summary: Create a reusable circular AssetBadgeView that uses the existing asset tint plus ticker text, then apply it consistently across app-wide asset display surfaces.

alternatives.title: Circular badges with abstract patterns
alternatives.summary: Add sector or asset-class patterns behind ticker text to make badges more visually distinct.
pros: More distinctive than color plus ticker alone
cons: Adds visual complexity and more QA burden
User explicitly selected color plus ticker only for this pass

alternatives.title: Rounded-square badge shape
alternatives.summary: Use rounded-square badges to increase ticker readability and create an app-icon-like look.
pros: Better room for longer ticker text
Can feel more distinctive in share surfaces
cons: Breaks from the approved circular direction
Less aligned with current result card badge language

alternatives.title: Official logo PNGs
alternatives.summary: Bundle real company or ETF logos and show them as asset icons.
cons: Requires logo sourcing and asset maintenance
Explicitly excluded by the approved intent

tradeoffs: The selected approach favors rights safety, consistency, and implementation speed over real-world brand recognition.
Color plus ticker is simpler than abstract patterns, but may be less visually rich.
Keeping the menu Picker preserves platform behavior but may limit how much badge detail appears in the collapsed selection control.
A reusable SwiftUI component is easier to scale and theme than PNG assets, but final visual polish depends on careful sizing and typography.
key_decisions: Use a shared AssetBadgeView rather than per-screen badge code.
Use Circle shape for all badge sizes.
Use existing AssetID.symbol, AssetID.displayName, and AssetID.tint as the data source.
Use color and ticker text only; do not add custom patterns in this pass.
Retain the existing menu Picker as the asset selection control.
Apply the shared badge to ExploreView and ShareCardView asset display surfaces.
assumptions: The current Xcode project uses synchronized filesystem groups, so adding a Swift file under the app target folder should not require manual project.pbxproj edits.
Existing asset tint values are acceptable starting colors for the badge system.
No new localized visible text is required if accessibility labels are built from existing symbol and displayName values.
A compact circular badge can remain legible with font scaling and line limits.

risks.description: Four- and five-letter symbols may be hard to read inside compact circular badges.
risks.mitigation: Define compact, standard, and hero sizes with scaled fonts, minimumScaleFactor, and tight line limits.

risks.description: SwiftUI menu Picker rendering may not preserve complex row content consistently across OS versions.
risks.mitigation: Keep the menu Picker and use the simplest supported custom row composition; if row badges render poorly, keep badge use around the picker label and menu text stable.

risks.description: Replacing small dots with ticker badges in dense areas may make the UI feel crowded.
risks.mitigation: Use compact sizing in comparison rows and timeline legend, and preserve existing spacing hierarchy.

risks.description: The share card renderer may expose sizing or clipping issues that are not obvious in regular views.
risks.mitigation: Verify ShareCardView rendering after badge integration.

validation_plan: Review code to confirm all replaced badges use the shared component.
Build the iOS app for simulator.
Run a visual simulator pass on the Explore screen with primary and comparison scenarios.
Render or preview the share card and inspect badge clipping and ticker legibility.
Search the repository for introduced PNG logo assets or official logo references.
